title: Is Addiction an Attachment Disorder?
content:
Is Addiction an Attachment Disorder?

SDG tags related to the news

DOI

https://doi.org/10.32739/uha.id.66475

Üsküdar Üniversitesi Founding Rector Psychiatrist Prof. Dr. Nevzat Tarhan attended the interview organized by the Istanbul Provincial Mufti's Office Addiction Control Coordination within the scope of ‘2026 Year of Independence’. In the interview titled “Mental State in Addicts”, Tarhan emphasized that the inability to meet the need for secure attachment lies at the root of addiction. He drew attention to the importance of strengthening the family structure. Stating that addiction is not only related to substance but also a process connected with the search for pleasure, the search for meaning, and psychological emptiness, Tarhan stated that protective approaches, long-term rehabilitation, and a meaning-oriented life are decisive in treatment.

The interview held at the Nermin Tarhan Conference Hall of Üsküdar Üniversitesi Central Campus was moderated by Bilgin Ekşi, Istanbul Provincial Coordinator for Combating Addiction.

Istanbul District Coordinators for Combating Addiction, led by Istanbul Deputy Provincial Mufti Zekeriya Bülbül, attended the program.

Zekeriya Bülbül: “We combine science with faith, sincerity with professionalism”

Istanbul Deputy Provincial Mufti Zekeriya Bülbül delivered the welcoming remarks for the program. Bülbül stated; “We embarked on this path perhaps with the sole intention of being Quran course instructors, but today we see that our Presidency of Religious Affairs undertakes very important responsibilities in every area of society. Our fundamental motto is, ‘Wherever there is religion and humanity, we must be there too.’ Today, especially in a vital field like combating addiction, we need to equip ourselves with scientific knowledge to guide our youth and society correctly. For this purpose, under the guidance of our esteemed Professor Nevzat Tarhan, we continue our journey by receiving training from experts in this field. The intense demand for coordinators from hospitals clearly shows how valuable and necessary our work in the field is. We are not just instructors but also guides who heal the wounds of society and extend a helping hand to our people. We thank our professors who opened their doors to us and shared their knowledge on this journey where we combine science with faith, and sincerity with professionalism.”

Prof. Dr. Nevzat Tarhan: “Inability to establish secure attachment forms the basis of addiction”

Üsküdar Üniversitesi Founding Rector Psychiatrist Prof. Dr. Nevzat Tarhan, stating that the basis of addiction relies on a need, began his words by saying; “Addiction and the need for attachment are actually very similar. Hormones related to this need are present in the human brain. They work differently in mothers and fathers. The first emotion a child feels at birth is fear. In the womb, there is a comfortable life, no effort is needed, everything is ready. Suddenly, they have to breathe, cold air fills their lungs, and this sudden change creates fear. They cry. Their first emotion is fear, and their first refuge is their mother. They attach to their mother; secure attachment begins this way. When a child can establish a secure attachment with their mother, this attachment expands over time. First to siblings, toys, life… As they grow, it turns to friends, their country, homeland, humanity, and finally to the Creator. The highest hierarchy of attachment is attachment to the Creator. Because the human brain is a relational brain; it has a need for attachment, and the process begins this way. In addiction, however, the process works differently. A significant portion of people initially turn to substances for pleasure, but after a while, they start taking substances not for pleasure, but to feel normal. For the first few weeks, there is a search for pleasure, then the substance becomes a means of feeling normal. Today, one of the most common reasons for substance use is not just curiosity or the search for excitement, but an attempt to reduce stress. Therefore, the psychological reason behind addiction is the inability of the person to meet their inner neurobiological need for attachment in a healthy and secure way. The inability to establish secure attachment forms the basis of addiction.”

“The greatest responsibility lies in strengthening the family structure…”

Tarhan, referring to the types of protection applied in treatments, said; “The human brain is born neuropsychologically premature. Consider a child who starts walking about a year after birth and reaches maturity to distinguish between good and bad by the age of 15. Now consider a duck; it can swim as soon as it hatches from the egg. The human brain, however, learns and develops through healthy and secure relationships it establishes with its environment. A person who cannot establish this relationship experiences an attachment disorder. For this reason, they turn to addiction, attach to objects, and try to relieve themselves with substances. Therefore, primary, secondary, and tertiary protective treatments are applied in treatment. Tertiary protection includes efforts made to prevent the patient from relapsing after treatment. This should largely be managed by the healthcare system. Secondary protection aims to identify risk groups and prevent relapse through early diagnosis. Supportive work is done to prevent the individual from returning to substance use. The most important is primary protection. This stage covers preventive and protective efforts made to ensure healthy individuals do not become addicted. Here, the greatest responsibility is towards strengthening and protecting the family structure.”

“People with depression constitute a risk group for addiction”

Tarhan, stating that antidepressant use is quite common globally, said; “If a person has childhood trauma, this situation can pave the way for a depressive structure and unhappiness in later years. When certain chemicals in the brain decrease, a person can be easily affected by even a small pleasure stimulus, become attached to it, and get carried away. For this reason, individuals who are particularly depressed constitute a risk group for addiction. Today, antidepressant use is quite widespread. Not only psychiatrists but also internists, gynecologists, and chest disease specialists can prescribe antidepressants. This situation is not unique to Turkey but reflects a global picture. Modernism and capitalism create a system that first makes people sick and then treats them.”

“The human eye is like a window opening to the material world of the soul”

Tarhan, likening the human soul (nafs) to a wild horse, said; “The human eye is like a window opening to the material world of the soul. For example, there is a painter named Eşref Armağan; he is blind but creates amazing paintings. They put him in an MRI machine, and studies show that in the brain of a blind person, the visual cortex and fingertips work similarly. He developed his fingertips so much that he practically activated the visual area of his brain, succeeding in imagining. So, in fact, it is not the eye that sees, but the brain. This was considered a revolutionary study in neuroscience. Our eyes are one of our most important organs, but there are also wild emotions in our inner world, the nafs desires. The nafs is like a wild horse. If you discipline it, it will take you where you want, but if you ignore it, you will be left stranded. This power placed within humans is like nuclear energy; if you use it in a good way, it produces energy, if you use it in a bad way, it turns into a destructive force.”

“Addicts are in search of something…”

Tarhan, underlining that addiction is not something that can be overcome by mere advice, said; “A significant portion of addicts are actually in search of something; they experience an inner void. They are unhappy, they are lonely. Addiction is not a situation that can be overcome with advice because the brain has transformed it into a state of captivity, an addiction process. Freedom should not be confused with irresponsibility. Instead of getting angry at these young people, we should approach them as victims, trying to rescue someone who has fallen into a swamp. Because what is at stake here is the brain being virtually hacked. Today, one of the concepts discussed, especially through the US economy, is the economy of pleasure. A pleasure-focused system is at play. Chemicals related to happiness in the brain are categorized under two main headings. One is dopamine, which is related to pleasure, short-term, fast, and mostly associated with material, concrete stimuli. The other is serotonin. Serotonin is related to meaning; it provides a longer-term and lasting state of well-being. The brain produces serotonin when it works in a meaning-oriented way. Antidepressant medications largely aim to increase serotonin levels in the brain. For this reason, antidepressants are frequently used in addiction treatment. The fundamental question here is, ‘Should one adopt a pleasure-oriented philosophy of life, or a meaning-oriented philosophy of life?’ The pleasure-oriented life philosophy says, ‘Everything should happen right now.’ Entertainment, eating and drinking, momentary pleasures… These produce dopamine. A meaning-oriented life, on the other hand, involves working, adding meaning to the work you produce, and even attributing meaning to the pain you experience. This approach supports serotonin production in the brain and provides long-term, deeper happiness.”

“In our tradition, an understanding focused on preserving health is more dominant”

Tarhan, stating that one should focus on the positive instead of fighting the negative, said; “The state has had many campaigns regarding cigarette use, laws have been enacted, yet despite this, we see that cigarette use is still very widespread. This means that it is not possible to achieve results by mere advice or intimidation. In approaching the people of this era, strengthening the positive rather than fighting the negative emerges as a more effective method. The approach adopted worldwide today is also in this direction. When I was a student, I bought and kept an Ottoman Turkish edition of the work called Tıbb-ı Nebevi, prepared by Doctor Hüseyin Remzi. Later, I also saw that it was translated into Turkish. When you examine this rare work, you realize that a large part of the book is based not so much on the treatment of diseases, but on how not to get sick. The definition given to medicine in the Ottoman Empire was also within this framework: preventive medicine. That is, the main issue was to protect health before treating it after getting sick. In contrast, Western medicine has emphasized its more curative aspect, focusing on fighting disease. In our tradition, however, an understanding focused on preserving health is more dominant. Indeed, when looking at the life and ethics of the Prophet, it is seen that most of his life advice is based on not getting sick and protecting one's health. This approach forms the basis of the protective and preventive health understanding.”

“Man sees life as only consisting of this world”

Tarhan, explaining the main symptoms of California Syndrome, said; “When describing the current state of humanity, we sometimes use the term California Syndrome. Why is it called California? Because it was first observed in California. Today, this situation has become a global syndrome. This syndrome has some basic characteristics. The first is hedonism, that is, pleasure-seeking. Pleasure is made the purpose of life. Since people do not think about what comes after death, they see life as consisting only of this world. No other period in human history has seen secularism so sanctified. An understanding that does not consider the day of reckoning has become widespread. When this happens, people can fall into the thought, ‘If no one will hold me accountable, I will do whatever I want.’ The second point is egoism. We are in an era where ego and narcissism are increasingly prevalent. In fact, studies and books are being published in America on this topic, suggesting a global epidemic of narcissism. Narcissism, selfishness transformed into a personality trait. The third consequence is loneliness. Today, Ministries of Loneliness have been established in the United Kingdom and Japan. People in old age can die alone in their homes, and no one may know for days. This picture shows a deep loneliness crisis experienced by the modern world. The Western world is in a serious depression. In fact, not only the West but the whole world is experiencing a search. If there is no major breaking point, humanity will eventually feel the need to rediscover the truth. At this point, the events in Gaza have also become a strong trigger for questioning and awareness for many people.”

“Where is the real one?”

Tarhan, stating that qualified companionship reduces risks, said; “Algorithms are not transparent. How the algorithms used by the media work is not fully known. Therefore, children under the age of 15 should especially not open social media accounts. If accounts have been opened, they must be protected. What we call media protection, filtering and content control appropriate to age and mental development level, should be provided. Children encounter information and images inappropriate for their development level at a very early age. This can negatively affect mental development. So, what will we do? We will not despair. If the family is a safe space, if the home offers a happy environment, if the parent-child relationship is warm and healthy, the child can recognize when encountering artificial and fake relationships outside. Even if they enter environments where substances are used and relationships are superficial, they can see that world is far from genuine and turn back to their family. When looking for ‘Where is the real one?’, they can choose their family as a safe harbor. For this reason, spending time with children at home is very important. Being a good business person is valuable, but raising a good child is a greater responsibility. Strengthening the bond within the family, dedicating quality time to the child, and establishing a warm relationship are among the strongest factors protecting them from the risks of the age.”

“The main issue is being able to sustain the 6-month rehabilitation process”

Tarhan, stating that the recovery of brain neuroplasticity is a time-consuming process, said; “Modern medical practices can often be helpless. You manage the acute phase, detoxification is done, a cleansing process is initiated, the patient leaves the hospital, but afterwards, tertiary protection is not sufficiently applied. The person is released, being told they are free. However, the recovery of the brain’s neuroplasticity is a time-consuming process. This is the point many of our colleagues overlook. The improvement in the brain takes approximately 6 months. The neural pathways in the brain are reshaped almost like a highway. We can think of it as if branched roads, like horns, have formed within the brain. In this sense, the deep narrative carried by some metaphors in certain hadiths is better understood. The acute phase passes in 2-3 weeks, and the person is discharged, but the truly important process begins after that. For this reason, we have developed scales on harm perception, consequence awareness, and life goals. We apply these three scales to the individual. If the level of consequence awareness, harm perception, and life purpose has reached a moderate or good level, we say, ‘This person has now learned what to do.’ If they manage to stay clean for 6 months, approximately 70% of the recovery takes place. Because when a person stays clean for 6 months, the brain largely begins to return to its former balance. Young people who successfully complete this period generally do not relapse easily. So, the main issue is being able to sustain that 6-month rehabilitation process. A significant reason for today’s failures is the inability to adequately continue this process.”

“The real advice is to be an example”

Tarhan, explaining how the sense of autonomy develops in children, said; “Children, as they progress towards adolescence around the age of 10, want to distance themselves somewhat from their parents and socialize. This is also biologically true; hormones become active, and the child begins to feel like an individual. They want to spend more time with friends. But if the family environment is healthy, they don't actually want to completely break away; they just want to be themselves, to feel their autonomy. At this point, the parents' attitude becomes decisive. An authoritarian approach was perhaps a model that worked in closed societies 100-200 years ago. Order could be maintained with fear and authority, but today the world has become an open society. Communication is open, access to information is unlimited. In this era, when building a relationship with a child, methods of persuasion, conviction, and making them love are effective, not pressure and intimidation. Advising is not just about talking face-to-face; the real advice is to be an example. Instead of constantly giving orders, one should offer choices. Instead of saying ‘Wear this, do that,’ offering a few alternatives is a healthier method. For example, you put a few clothes in front of the child, and when they choose one, they experience the feeling of ‘I chose it.’ This way, the need for autonomy is satisfied, and the parents do not completely lose control. This approach should actually start at a young age, even before 10. Offering choices to the child and making them feel that they made their own decision supports both personality development and strengthens their bond with the family.”

“The most important thing to do is to warm people’s hearts”

Tarhan, emphasizing that the most powerful message is being a good example, concluded his words by saying; “It is especially necessary to take this issue of addiction to heart. Because today, a significant portion of people have confused right and wrong. In such a period, actions are more effective than words. People of this era look not at what you say, but at what you do. Therefore, the most powerful message is to be a good example. If we are good examples, people will be influenced by it. Indeed, when looking at the early periods of Prophet Muhammad’s prophethood, we see that he devoted all his energy to making people love Islam. First, to warm hearts, to bring people closer… This was the essential method. Therefore, the most important thing to do is to warm people’s hearts. Doing good, building trust, being a good example… Thus, religion is conveyed through love, not fear. Making people love the Quran and the Prophet’s ethics is the most powerful and lasting way to have an impact.”

After the interview, a gift was presented to Tarhan.

The program ended with a group photo session.

Üsküdar News Agency (ÜHA)
